channel.title: Blog 1 on Brennan Ryan Profile
channel.description: Recent content in Blog 1 on Brennan Ryan Profile

item.title: Blog 1
item.description:
An Implication of Ethical Complication Aristotle’s Nicomachean Ethic states that moral behaviour is between 2 extremes:

  • Excess (using all available data, without ethical concern)
  • Deficiency (not using any data at all) Both of these extremes pose significant drawbacks, the former (excess) being unethical and risking any and all privacy of data. The latter (deficiency) being so cautious as to not gather data at all prevents any form of progress and innovation. This is where a concept known as the ethics equilibrium comes into play, a balance between the two extremes so as to foster growth while protecting the data privacy of everyone involved.

An Ethical Conundrum At the completion of a course, participants are given a survey of 10 questions. To perform the survey, participants access a URL in their web browser that is made available for a short period of time and requires that students log in with their username and password for the organization. The beginning of the survey prompts for the name of the course, but not their name. Instead, the survey description on this page indicates that all answers and the survey itself will remain anonymous. Executive management wishes to use the results of the survey to determine which courses require redesign or updating. You are the data analyst assigned to this task and are required to provide actionable insights for this purpose to executive management. Upon reading this you may be asking where the ethical concerns are? If at all? Here is my angle on the situation: Participants are required to login to complete the survey, while anonymity is promised, it may still be possible for executive management to determine the identity of each participant via the data provided by the participant logging in by cross-referencing the data available through the participant’s logins elsewhere on the same account. This would violate the promise of privacy safety and as well open the door to prejudice and bias where perhaps the executive management would prefer to take action depending on their preferred participants rather than working with anonymous data.
